description:
You are a compliance analyst who has to generate an auditable checksum-verification trail for two quarterly finance files that are already on the system.

    1. **Files that must be verified**
       • /home/user/documents/finance_Q1_2024.csv  
       • /home/user/documents/finance_Q2_2024.csv  

    2. **Reference hashes**
       A file holding the authoritative SHA-256 sums is already provided at  
       /home/user/reference/hashes.sha256  
       The file uses the canonical “sha256sum” output style:
       <64-hex-chars><two_spaces><relative_filename>

    3. **What you must do**
       a. Re-compute the SHA-256 for each target file.  
       b. Compare every freshly calculated value with the corresponding value in /home/user/reference/hashes.sha256.  
       c. For each file, append exactly one audit line to /home/user/audit/checksum_audit.log using the format shown below.

    4. **Required audit-trail line format**  
       Each line must be append-only (do **not** overwrite previous entries) and follow this template *precisely* (pipe-separated, single spaces around the “|” symbols):

       YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS | <basename> | status:<MATCH_or_MISMATCH> | calculated:<64-hex-chars> | expected:<64-hex-chars>

       • YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS must be the local system time at the moment the line is written.  
       • <basename> is just the filename (e.g. finance_Q1_2024.csv).  
       • status must be the exact uppercase literal MATCH or MISMATCH.

       Example (layout only – your hashes and timestamp will differ):
       2024-06-04 11:22:53 | finance_Q1_2024.csv | status:MATCH | calculated:aaaaaaaa… | expected:aaaaaaaa…

    5. **Success criteria**  
       • /home/user/audit/checksum_audit.log exists.  
       • It contains exactly two *new* lines, one per file, conforming to the format above.  
       • The calculated and expected hashes on each line are identical to those obtained from running `sha256sum` on the corresponding file and reading /home/user/reference/hashes.sha256.  
       • status:MATCH appears on every newly added line (because the reference values are correct).

    Only typical command-line utilities available on a minimal Linux install may be used.  
    Do not hard-code the hashes into the audit file; derive them programmatically from the files themselves.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing artefacts -->
    1. /home/user/documents/finance_Q1_2024.csv        (empty file; size 0)
    2. /home/user/documents/finance_Q2_2024.csv        (empty file; size 0)

    The SHA-256 of an empty file is:
    e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    3. /home/user/reference/hashes.sha256  (text file, mode 0644)
       Contents — two lines, each terminated with a single newline:
       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  finance_Q1_2024.csv
       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  finance_Q2_2024.csv

    4. /home/user/audit/ directory exists (mode 0755, owned by user).

    <!-- Post-execution expectations -->
    After the agent finishes:
    • /home/user/audit/checksum_audit.log exists and is world-readable (0644).  
    • Exactly two *new* lines have been appended; they match this regular expression (POSIX-extended):

      ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} \| finance_Q[12]_2024\.csv \| status:MATCH \| calculated:e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855 \| expected:e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855$

    • The chronological order of the two lines does not matter.  
    • No other modifications are made to the files in /home/user/documents/ or /home/user/reference/.
